title: Architecting mechanosensitive nanofluidic transport in graphite nanoslits
authors: Mathieu Lizée, Zhijia Zhang, Baptiste Coquinot, Qian Yang, Lydéric Bocquet
abstract: Mechanosensitive ion transport plays a central role in enabling living systems to perceive and adapt to their environment through the deformation of soft, embedded ion channels. In this work, we demonstrate that ion transport within a two-dimensional graphite nanoslit can be rationally engineered to achieve a bipolar, pressure-sensitive response without any structural deformation. The mechanosensitivity arises from the selective charging of one channel inlet, which acts as a reversible source of mobile charge carriers. These excess-ions can then be advected in or out of the channel by the pressure-driven water flow, thereby modulating the ionic conductance. This mechanism is captured through a comprehensive electrohydrodynamic model that analytically accounts for coupled diffusion, convection, surface transport, diffusio-osmosis, and interfacial slippage, both inside and outside the nanoslit. The theoretical framework quantitatively reproduces the experimental data, showing that a simple surface charge pattern can give rise to complex, pressure-dependent conductance. These findings reveal how rich nonlinear couplings at the nanoscale can be harnessed to design adaptive, bioinspired nanofluidic systems, exemplified here by ionic pressure sensors.
summary: The researchers engineered a graphite-based nanofluidic device that changes its electrical conductance in response to pressure without any physical deformation of the channel. This is achieved by creating an asymmetric surface charge at the channel inlet, which allows pressure-driven flow to move ions into or out of the slit. This work provides a blueprint for designing bioinspired, solid-state pressure sensors.
main_problem: Developing artificial, strain-free mechanosensitive nanofluidic systems that mimic biological ion channels by achieving pressure-dependent conductance without structural deformation.
main_result: Demonstrated that asymmetric surface charging at a graphite nanoslit inlet enables a bipolar, pressure-sensitive ionic conductance response driven by the advection of excess ions.
method: The study combines van der Waals assembly and plasma treatment for device fabrication with electrochemical measurements and a 1D electrohydrodynamic EDL-advection model.
model_or_system: The physical system consists of 2D graphite nanoslits (height 1-7 nm) with an activated (highly charged) inlet, placed between reservoirs of aqueous KCl solution.
key_observables: Relative conductance change (delta G/G0), ionic current (I), streaming current (Istr), and ion concentration profiles.
important_parameters: Pressure drop (delta P), salt concentration (c0), channel height (h), surface charge density (Sigma), and slip length (b).
main_assumptions: Assumes a 1D EDL-advection model, negligible channel wall deformation, large slip length (b >> h), and specific power-law scaling for surface charge regulation.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the experimental setup, pressure convention, and the bipolar conductance response in activated vs. pristine channels. Fig 2 illustrates the electrohydrodynamic model. Fig 3 and 4 present the concentration-dependent mechanosensitivity and the scaling of the response with channel height.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the concept of strain-free mechanosensitivity, describes the fabrication and experimental setup, presents the observed bipolar conductance response, provides a theoretical 1D model to explain the mechanism via ion advection, and analyzes the scaling of the effect with salt concentration and channel height.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Mechano-sensitive nanochannels experimental setup. (a-b) A two-dimensional graphite nanochannel fabricated by van der Waals assembly is placed between two reservoirs filled with aqueous KCl solution. A voltage drop is applied between two Ag/AgCl electrodes places in two reservoirs on either side of the channel. A pressure drop is also applied between the two reservoirs. By convention, the pressure drop is positive when the pressure is applied on the top side of the nanochannel, as indicated by the arrow on top. (c) The measured current from a 1 nm activated channel in 1 M KCl solution under both positive and negative pressure drop steps and a voltage drop ∆V = 100 mV. (d) The...

Figure 2. An electrohydrodynamic model for mechano-sensitivity(a) Sketch of the model: counter charges near the activated inlet are advected by the water flow with applied pressure, resulting in a pressure-dependent concentration profile in the channel. Darker color represents higher concentration (b) Theoretical predictions for the pressure-dependent relative concentration drop profiles in a 1-nm-height channel plotted for h = 1 nm, L = 5 µm, b = 20 nm, Σch = −25 mC/m2 and Σinlet = −150 mC/m2 at c0 = 1 M, closely matching experimental conditions. (c) Experimental results for the mechano- conductance of 1-nm-height channels with respectively a pristine (red) and an activated inlet (black),...

Figure 3. Concentration dependency of the mechano-sensitive effect and fits with the theoretical model (a) Mechano-conductance versus pressure curves for a channel with height h = 2.4 nm for ∆P ranging between 0 and 1 bar. Solid lines are a 1-parameter fit with the theoretical model, assuming a slip length of 20 nm, a pristine graphite surface charge of -25 mC/m2 at c0=1 M and a regulation of Σch ∼c1/3 0 consistent with existing measurements on carbon nanotubes (23). (b) Surface charge Σinlet for each ion concentration extracted from the fits of the theoretical predictions for various channel height (see Supplementary Figure 9). We find that the data for all confinements collapse on a...

Figure 4. Relative mechanosensitive response as a function of salt concentration for a range of pressure drops in channels of different heights. (a-c) Concentration-dependent mechanosensitivity curves for channels of different height h ∈{1, 2.4, 4} nm for a set of pressures ranging between 0 and 1 bar. The same dataset is replotted in Supplementary Figure 9 as a function of pressure instead of concentration. (d-f) Relative mechanosensitivity ∆G/G0(c0, h, ∆P) plotted according to the constrained one-dimensional EDL advection model without any free parameter. We take b = 20 nm, L = 5 µm, Σinlet = −200 mC/m2 × (c0/1M)0.7 and Σch = −25 mC/m2 × (c0/1M)0.3.
